worth_it_explanation: Conversion value is the number that target return on ad spend (target ROAS) Smart Bidding optimizes toward. A missing or inaccurate value means the bid strategy is steering toward a signal it cannot use, or worse, a signal that is actively wrong. These checks take a short time to run using the conversion action settings and a Tag Assistant recording, and the fixes are configuration changes in the tag rather than structural rebuilds.
intro: Conversion value is the monetary figure Google Ads records alongside each conversion. It feeds value-based Smart Bidding strategies such as target return on ad spend (target ROAS) and Maximize conversion value, and it appears in reported metrics including conversion value, value per conversion, and return on ad spend (ROAS). These checks confirm that the tag passes a real, transaction-level value, that the currency is specified correctly, and that any value rules in use reflect deliberate business decisions rather than configuration drift.

audit_checks.title: Conversion actions record no value, so value-based Smart Bidding has no signal to optimize toward
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When value is missing, target ROAS and Maximize conversion value strategies have no revenue signal, so they fall back on conversion count alone. The account may be spending heavily toward low-value conversions while underinvesting in high-value ones, and no report can show the difference because the data is not there.

educate.base:
Google Ads records a conversion event when the conversion tag fires. Alongside that event, the tag can pass a value, the revenue or assigned worth of that conversion, and a currency code. When value is present, bidding strategies such as target return on ad spend (target ROAS) and Maximize conversion value can optimize bids toward the conversions that are worth the most, not only toward the most conversions.

When value is absent, those strategies have no revenue signal. They cannot distinguish a $5 lead from a $500 one. The account reported return on ad spend (ROAS) metric reads zero or is unavailable, which removes one of the cleaner ways to measure whether the account is profitable. Smart Bidding defaults to volume when value is missing, which is a different optimization objective than what those strategies were chosen for.

Value goes missing for a few reasons. The conversion tag may have been set up without a dynamic value parameter, leaving the field empty on every conversion. The page may pass a static placeholder such as '1' rather than the real order amount. Or the tag may fire before the order amount loads on the page, so the value arrives empty even when the variable is configured.

You can confirm the issue by looking at two places. In the Google Ads UI, the conversion action's average conversion value column shows zero or is absent when no value is recorded. In Google Tag Assistant, the conversion event recorded during a test purchase shows whether the value parameter is present and populated.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the transaction total is available on the order confirmation page at the moment the conversion fires. You may want to confirm that the tag reads the actual order amount rather than a static placeholder, and that the order total loads before the tag fires. A value of '1' on every purchase is a sign the placeholder was never replaced with the dynamic variable.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a real monetary value is not available at the moment of form submission, so assigning an estimated value per lead is an accepted approach. You may want to confirm that an estimated value is assigned in the conversion action settings rather than left empty. Even an approximate figure lets target ROAS and Maximize conversion value strategies run as intended.

learn_more.source_title: Use the same value for all conversions

investigate.intro: Check whether the conversion action records a non-zero value and confirm the tag passes a real transaction-level value rather than a placeholder or nothing.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and find the conversion action you want to check.
Add the 'Conv. value / conv.' column to the table if it is not already visible. A value of 0.00 or a dash in that column means the action records no value.
Open the conversion action settings and check the 'Value' field. If it is set to 'Don't assign a value', the tag sends no value and value-based bidding cannot use this action.

google_tag_assistant.label: Google Tag Assistant
steps: Open Tag Assistant, start a recording, and complete a test conversion on the site, for example by completing a purchase.
Find the conversion event in the recording and expand it. Look for a 'value' parameter in the event data.
If the value parameter is absent, empty, or shows '1' on a transaction page where the real amount is available, the tag is not passing a meaningful value.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per primary conversion action, whether a non-zero conversion value appears in the 'Conv. value / conv.' column and whether Tag Assistant shows a populated value parameter during a test conversion. A conversion action with a real transaction-level value in both places passes. A conversion action with a zero, empty, or static placeholder value is a fail, recorded with the conversion action name and the observed placeholder or empty value.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the conversion value column reading and the Tag Assistant value parameter for each primary conversion action above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s primary conversion actions record no value or record a static placeholder, which means value-based Smart Bidding strategies such as target ROAS and Maximize conversion value have no revenue signal to optimize toward. Without a real transaction-level value, these strategies default to optimizing for conversion count regardless of what each conversion is worth. {{voice_recommend}} updating the conversion tag to pass the actual order amount dynamically on the confirmation page, confirming the value loads before the tag fires, and verifying in Google Tag Assistant that the value parameter is populated during a test conversion. For lead-generation actions where no transaction value is available, {{voice_recommend}} assigning an estimated value per lead in the conversion action settings.

audit_checks.title: Conversions pass a static value where a dynamic per-transaction value should be used
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A static value makes every sale appear identical in Google Ads. The reported ROAS is the ratio of a made-up number to real spend, and target ROAS has no basis for preferring a $500 sale over a $20 one. The bid strategy and the reports both look fine on the surface while the underlying data is wrong.

educate.base:
Google Ads allows a conversion action to send a fixed value with each conversion or a dynamic value that changes per transaction. For a purchase event, the dynamic value is the actual order total. When a fixed value is used instead, Google Ads records the same revenue figure for every sale, regardless of what the customer actually spent.

The effect on bidding is direct. Target return on ad spend (target ROAS) compares the value Google Ads records to the cost of the clicks that led to it. If the value is always the same fixed amount, the strategy cannot tell which keywords, audiences, or placements produce high-revenue sales versus low-revenue ones. It treats a $500 transaction as identical to a $20 transaction. Bid adjustments that should favor high-value traffic have no basis for doing so.

The effect on reporting is the same. The conversion value column and the ROAS metric both reflect the fixed amount multiplied by the number of conversions. The number looks real, but it has no connection to actual revenue. Comparing campaigns by ROAS under these conditions does not answer which campaign earns more revenue.

The fix is replacing the fixed value in the tag or Google Tag Manager (GTM) configuration with a variable that reads the transaction total from the data layer or the page. Most e-commerce platforms expose the order amount on the confirmation page. A short tag configuration change swaps the static number for that variable.

You can identify the problem by checking two places. In Google Ads, the 'Conv. value / conv.' column shows an identical amount across a large number of conversions, which is unlikely for any real store's order mix. In Google Tag Assistant, the value parameter in the conversion event stays the same across test purchases of different amounts.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the clearest sign of a static value is the 'Conv. value / conv.' column showing the same round number, such as 1.00 or 10.00, across thousands of transactions. You may want to compare that figure against the average order value from a separate analytics source. If they differ, the tag is not passing real transaction data. The data layer on the confirmation page is the usual source for the dynamic variable.

learn_more.source_title: Set up conversion tracking for websites

investigate.intro: Check whether the conversion action records a realistic spread of values or the same amount on every conversion.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and find the purchase or transaction conversion action.

Add the 'Conv. value / conv.' column to the table. If the value is a round number such as 1.00 or 10.00 and does not vary across a period with many conversions, the tag is likely sending a fixed amount.

Open the conversion action settings and look at the 'Value' field. If it is set to a specific fixed number rather than 'Use a different value for each conversion', the action is configured for static values.

google_tag_assistant.label: Google Tag Assistant
steps: Start a Tag Assistant recording and complete two test purchases with different order totals.

Compare the value parameter in the conversion event for each purchase. If the value is the same despite different cart totals, the tag is sending a fixed amount rather than reading the transaction total.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the 'Conv. value / conv.' column shows a realistic spread of values and whether Tag Assistant shows different values for purchases of different amounts. A conversion action showing a realistic spread with different values per transaction in Tag Assistant passes. A conversion action showing a constant value across many conversions or showing the same amount in Tag Assistant regardless of cart total is a fail, recorded with the conversion action name and the static amount observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the 'Conv. value / conv.' reading and the Tag Assistant values from two test purchases of different amounts above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s purchase conversion action passes a static value rather than the actual transaction total with each conversion. The reported return on ad spend (ROAS) reflects that fixed amount multiplied by the number of conversions rather than real revenue, and target ROAS Smart Bidding has no basis for preferring high-value transactions over low-value ones. {{voice_recommend}} updating the conversion tag or Google Tag Manager configuration to read the order total from the data layer or the confirmation page and pass it as a dynamic value with each conversion event. After the change, {{voice_recommend}} completing two test purchases of different amounts and confirming in Google Tag Assistant that the value parameter reflects each transaction total.

audit_checks.title: Currency is missing or mismatched, so values from different markets are compared as one
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When currency codes are absent or wrong, Google Ads adds values across currencies using a single rate or no rate, producing a total that mixes different units. ROAS targets set against that total are met or missed for reasons that have nothing to do with campaign performance.

educate.base:
When a conversion tag fires, it can send a currency code alongside the value, for example 'USD', 'EUR', or 'GBP'. That code tells Google Ads which currency the value is in. Google Ads uses it to convert values to the account currency when the two differ, so that the total conversion value column reflects a consistent unit across all transactions.

Two problems appear when the currency handling is wrong. The first is a missing currency code. When no currency is sent, Google Ads assumes the value is in the account's default currency. If the site sells in multiple currencies and the tag omits the code, a 90-euro purchase and a 90-dollar purchase are treated as identical, and the total is a sum of numbers that are not the same units.

The second is a mismatched currency. If the conversion tag always sends 'USD' but the confirmation page shows prices in the user's local currency, the value in Google Ads is denominated in dollars while the actual transaction was in, say, euros. The total conversion value can appear inflated or deflated depending on which currency is stronger, and the ROAS calculation reflects the mismatch.

You can check the currency a conversion action uses in the account settings. Google Ads shows the currency on the conversion action detail page. You may want to compare it against the currency or currencies your store transacts in. For sites that sell in multiple currencies, you may also want to confirm the tag sends the transaction-specific currency code with each conversion rather than a single hard-coded value.

The Google Ads conversion value documentation covers the currency parameter and how Google Ads handles currency conversion.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store that sells to customers in more than one country, the tag may need to send a dynamic currency code that matches the transaction, not a hard-coded string. You may want to confirm the data layer exposes the transaction currency and that the tag reads it, rather than always sending the account's default currency regardless of where the customer is.

learn_more.source_title: Track sales and other conversions from your website

investigate.intro: Check whether the conversion action is set to the correct currency and whether the tag sends a currency code that matches the transaction.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Goals', then 'Conversions', and select the purchase conversion action.

In the conversion action detail page, note the currency listed. Confirm it matches the currency your store actually transacts in, or in the case of multiple currencies, that the tag will handle the mismatch dynamically.

If the account-level currency differs from the transaction currency and no dynamic currency code is passed with conversions, a currency conversion is being applied that may not match the actual exchange rate at the time of the transaction.

google_tag_assistant.label: Google Tag Assistant
steps: Start a Tag Assistant recording and complete a test purchase.
Find the conversion event and look for a 'currency' parameter in the event data.
Confirm the currency code present in the event, such as 'USD', 'EUR', or 'GBP', matches the currency the store charged the customer for that transaction.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the currency shown in the conversion action settings and the currency code present in the conversion event seen in Tag Assistant. If the store transacts in one currency, a match between the conversion action currency and the transaction currency passes. If the store transacts in multiple currencies and the tag sends a hard-coded single currency rather than a dynamic code, that is a fail, recorded with the conversion action name and the currency mismatch observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the conversion action currency setting and the currency code from a Tag Assistant recording above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} a currency mismatch in {{site_name}}'s conversion tracking. The conversion tag sends a currency code that does not match the transactions it records, or it omits the currency code entirely and relies on the account default. When values in different currencies are added without the correct currency code, the total conversion value column reflects mixed units, and any return on ad spend (ROAS) target set against that total is measured against a number that does not represent real revenue in a consistent currency. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the currency code sent with each conversion matches the currency of the transaction, and for sites that sell in multiple currencies, {{voice_recommend}} updating the tag to pass the transaction currency dynamically rather than a hard-coded value.
